title: 3 Hidden Dangers of Non-reversible Filler
excerpt: So a long needle with a slight angle could end up placing that filler three four seven millimeters closer to the archery you're trying to avoid if you're using a product that is as thick as rds or calcium hydroxyapatite in general it simply will not aspirate there is it's like co…

segments.text: so a long needle with a slight angle could end up placing that filler three four seven millimeters closer to the archery you're trying to avoid if you're using a product that is as thick as rds or calcium hydroxyapatite in general it simply will not aspirate there is it's like concrete once it's in the needle you will never get any product back out of it um now i suppose you could use a non-primed needle but i doubt in those days aspiration wasn't spoken about as much there probably was no aspiration either and the other thing about it is being quite a thick product i remember from using myself it requires quite a high degree of extrusion force and i remember sensing that this was a something that took away some of my control so as i'm injecting you wanted to go in nothing's happening you're squeezing harder and then suddenly it gives so i never like that about that particular product it's like an inertia with the product and of course highlays doesn't work as the key one which we've covered already the next thing that might be relevant is the instrument now this i don't believe was the case in julie's case because she says it was a

three-quarter needle which is similar to what we already used three-quarter inch and but i know that sometimes that product and some of these products come with longer needles and as i touched on last time i think that multiplies in accuracy so if you're injecting quite a thick fleshy part of the face um remember what every injector is doing as they put the needle in as they're mentally projecting where that needle is going and the further the deeper it goes in the longer the needle the greater the room for error and i certainly having trained thousands of clinicians you quite often will find clinicia clinicians at different stages have different levels of accuracy and they think it's in one place this happens a lot with cannulas but it turns out it's actually in a considerably different place so we need to be maximizing the resolution of our anatomical knowledge but we also need to be being certain that our instruments improve our accuracy so a long needle with a slight angle could end up placing that filler three four seven millimeters closer to the archery you're trying to avoid with it looking fairly similar
to the injector so short needles are probably safer that's one more potential risk factor we could take from this case

keyPoints.text: So a long needle with a slight angle could end up placing that filler three four seven millimeters closer to the archery you're trying to avoid if you're using a product that is as thick as rds or calcium hydroxyapatite …
